questions.question: What does the classical capacity C of a quantum channel measure?
questions.explanation: Classical capacity C measures the maximum rate of classical bits that can be reliably transmitted per use of the quantum channel.

questions.question: What is the coherent information used for?
questions.explanation: Coherent information Ic = S(B) - S(E) is the key quantity for quantum capacity. Q = lim max Ic(ρ, ε⊗n)/n.

questions.question: What does it mean if a channel is 'anti-degradable'?
questions.explanation: Anti-degradable channels satisfy Bob's channel = Eve's channel ∘ D. The environment always gets at least as much information as Bob, so Q = 0.

questions.question: What is superadditivity of channel capacity?

options.text: Two channels together can have more capacity than sum

questions.explanation: Superadditivity means C(ε₁⊗ε₂) > C(ε₁) + C(ε₂) is possible. Using channels together with entangled inputs can exceed individual capacities.

questions.question: How does the entanglement-assisted capacity CE compare to classical capacity C?

options.text: CE is always less than C
CE is always at least C (CE ≥ C)

questions.explanation: Entanglement-assisted capacity CE ≥ C always. Pre-shared entanglement can only help (or make no difference), never hurt classical transmission.

questions.question: What is the quantum capacity of an erasure channel with erasure probability p = 0.6?
questions.explanation: For erasure channels, Q = max(0, 1-2p). When p ≥ 0.5, the quantum capacity is zero. At p = 0.6, Q = max(0, -0.2) = 0.

questions.question: Computing the quantum capacity of an arbitrary channel is computationally easy.
questions.explanation: False. Computing quantum capacity is extremely hard - in general it's undecidable! The regularization required and superadditivity make it computationally intractable for most channels.
